content:
# Matthew Chapter 28 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_028_page_001.jpg, MAT_028_page_002.jpg

1. And after [the] Sabbaths, at the dawning toward the first [day] of [the] week, came Maria the Magdalene and the other Maria to see the grave.

2. And behold, a great earthquake happened, for an angel of [the] Lord, having descended from heaven and having come, rolled away the stone and was sitting upon it.

3. And the appearance of him was as lightning, and the clothing of him white as snow.

4. And from the fear of him the [ones] guarding trembled and became as dead [men].

5. And answering, the angel said to the women, "Do not fear, for I know that you seek the Yeshua, the [one] having been crucified.

6. He is not here, for he was raised just as he said. Come, see the place where he was lying.

7. And going quickly, tell the disciples of him that he was raised from the dead, and behold, he goes before you into the Galilaia; there you will see him. Behold, I have told you."

8. And going away quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy, they ran to report to the disciples of him.

9. And behold, the Yeshua met them, saying, "Greetings!" And they, coming, seized the feet of him and worshiped him.

10. Then the Yeshua says to them, "Do not fear. Go, report to the brothers of me that they should go to the Galilaia, and there they will see me."

11. And [while] they were going, behold, some of the guard, having come into the city, reported to the chief priests all the things having happened.

12. And having been gathered with the elders and having taken counsel, they gave sufficient silver to the soldiers,

13. saying, "Say, 'The disciples of him, having come by night, stole him [while] we were sleeping.'

14. And if this is heard before the governor, we will persuade him and make you free from care."

15. And they, having taken the silver, did as they were instructed. And this word was spread among [the] Ioudaioi until the today day.

16. And the eleven disciples went to the Galilaia, to the mountain where the Yeshua appointed them.

17. And seeing him, they worshiped, but some doubted.

18. And coming, the Yeshua spoke to them, saying, "All authority was given to me in heaven and upon earth.

19. Going therefore, make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,

20. teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I commanded you. And behold, I am with you all the days until the completion of the age."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "after the Sabbaths":** ὀψὲ δὲ σαββάτων — the plural σαββάτων ("Sabbaths") is a standard Greek rendering of the Hebrew שַׁבָּת. The timing indicates the early morning transition from Sabbath to the first day of the week.

2. **v.6 — "he was raised":** ἠγέρθη — passive voice. Not "he rose" (active) but "he was raised" — by God. This is the consistent NT pattern: the Father raises the Son. The passive preserves the theological agency.

3. **v.9 — "Greetings":** χαίρετε — literally "rejoice!" The same word used by Judas in 26:49. The ironic contrast is powerful: Judas used the greeting to betray; the risen Jesus uses it to restore.

4. **v.17 — "but some doubted":** οἱ δὲ ἐδίστασαν — a remarkable admission. Even at the climactic resurrection appearance, some among the eleven hesitated. Matthew does not sanitize the response. The Greek δίσταζω means "to be of two minds" — the same word used of Peter's wavering on the water (14:31).

5. **v.19 — "make disciples of all the nations":** μαθητεύσατε πάντα τὰ ἔθνη — the main imperative is μαθητεύσατε ("make disciples"). The participles "going," "baptizing," and "teaching" describe how discipleship happens. The scope is πάντα τὰ ἔθνη — "all the nations/gentiles" — the universal mission that was anticipated throughout Matthew (2:1-12; 8:11; 24:14; 25:32).

6. **v.20 — "the completion of the age":** συντελείας τοῦ αἰῶνος — the same phrase from 13:39-40, 49 and 24:3. Matthew's Gospel ends where it began: with Emmanuel, "God with us" (1:23). The final word is presence.

— EMET, AI Translator
